policy_statement: Sustainable Buildings: achieve net-zero carbon for all new buildings, reduce emissions by 25% for existing buildings, and lower natural gas-related emissions by 30% by 2030.
mechanism_description: Mandating net-zero standards for new construction and improved performance in existing buildings reduces operational energy use and associated on-site fossil fuel (natural gas) combustion emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Higher building energy performance delivers the same building services (heating/cooling/lighting) with less energy input, inherently improving energy productivity even when grid emissions are low.
primary_causal_pathway: Building design/construction standards → ↓ building energy demand (and often ↓ on-site fuel combustion) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Policies require or induce more efficient building envelopes, HVAC, lighting, controls, and sometimes electrification/net-zero performance for new (and sometimes existing) buildings.  
(2) Mediating process: Improved thermal performance and equipment efficiency reduce delivered electricity and fuel needed to provide indoor comfort and services; where fossil systems are reduced, direct combustion also falls.  
(3) Outcome: Lower operational CO₂e emissions (and frequently lower peak load), contributing to climate change mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced operational energy demand in buildings, which lowers associated CO₂e emissions from electricity and on-site fuel use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: building standards → reduced energy use → reduced operational GHG emissions (largest and most direct effect).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While codes can incidentally improve comfort/heat resilience, the defining and generally largest intended climate impact across instances is emissions reduction from lower energy consumption and fuel switching.  
3) Inherent secondary chain(s):  
   - Resource Efficiency is inherent because the mechanism is explicitly about delivering the same building function with less energy input.  
4) Edge cases applied: If the electricity grid is already zero-carbon, mitigation benefits shrink but the mechanism still targets operational emissions in general and remains primarily mitigation across typical contexts; adaptation co-benefits (e.g., better thermal comfort during heat/outages) are not guaranteed and thus not inherent.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy that tightens building energy performance to reduce energy use will, by construction, reduce energy demand and typically emissions (Mitigation) and always improve energy input efficiency (Resource Efficiency).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In very low-carbon grids or where efficiency leads to rebound effects, net GHG reductions may be smaller; adaptation benefits (reduced heat stress, improved passive survivability) can occur but depend on design specifics and are not universal to all energy-code policies.
key_indicators: "By 2030"; "achieve net-zero carbon for all new buildings"; "reduce emissions by 25% for existing buildings"; "lowering all natural gas-related emissions by 30%"
co_benefits: Potential air quality/health benefits from reduced natural gas combustion; potential energy cost savings for occupants; potential reduced peak load (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could be implemented via performance standards or building code mandates, but the text itself is framed as an outcome/goal without specifying the enforcing mechanism; thus classified as target-setting and indirect.
